Because before `malloc` you may already have allocated other resources. Or maybe you didn't, but you change your code later and open a fd or use another `malloc` before this one, or create a Python list or whatever.
The "free" logic should just live in one place (`error:`) so that every time you want to raise an exception you set the error type (`PyErr_NoMemory();` or whatever) and then just do `goto error`.
This way you don't have to reason about what resources you allocated thus far and you don't have to infer whether to use `goto errmem`, `goto oserror` etc.

I've actually considered all of this, and have waffled back and forth between having it be a decorator and a simple two lines of code at the end of every function it's needed in. I liked the decorator approach better than calling a member function at the end of every function. I also considered only putting it on user-facing functions, but I chose to put it everywhere because of the possibility that users will call private functions. I couldn't find a case where having these checks on functions called on worker threads actually causes a problem - the thread will not have a chance to raise the exception a second time from the decorator, since it will already have exited after the original exception (from the `break` in its main loop error handler). I've also not been able to replicate a case where having `exit()` decorated causes a problem, but that's the one I'm least confident about.

Since a lot of what you've said here aligns with my gut instinct about this, I believe I'll keep the decorator on public methods only, unless I've inadvertently convinced you otherwise here.

It happens if the user tries to set an input that doesn't have the same `dtype` than the variable declared `dtype`.

The typically "innocent" case this code bit handle is:

 ```py
holder.set_input(period, np.asarray([200, 400]))
```

Signals that occur if `__exit__` is triggered due to an exception other than `SignalExit` are completely ignored. This should be fixed.

A nice way to do this I think is to refactor this function a bit to have a form like the following:
```
self.body_executed = True
retval = False
if exec_type in (None, SystemExit):
    return retval
elif exec_type is SignalExit:
    log signals
    retVal = True
else:
    log exception

call_registered
call_signals
reset_signals
return retVal
```

This was discussed with a lot of detail in #4433. Basically our plan is not to specify the maximum version of our dependencies on PyPI as when new versions of our dependencies are released and packaged in OSes, Certbot package maintainers have to do extra work to remove these requirements.

Instead, the plan is to continue pinning specific versions of dependencies in `certbot-auto` and add this to our developer set up so temporary problems with new versions of upstream dependencies don't slow down Certbot contributors. We have other tests that test with the newest versions of all our dependencies so core developers will still be notified when changes from upstream break things.

This was one of the fragile and weakest parts of CoT v1, looking at the decision task and trying to spot check it to make sure that it looked vaguely like a decision task. It's complex, fragile, and doesn't actually protect us from all decision task changes. CoT v2 added json-e rebuilding of the decision task, so we verify the entirety of the decision task comes from `.taskcluster.yml`, not just the command. [bug 1395413](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1395413) also describes some reasons why this command check is not ideal.

This was another part of CoT v1 that we were very interested in sunsetting when everything supported CoT v2.

By registering a single token network event listener/filter with `None` as topics, we get every event on the TokenNetwork. `OPENED` events must be handled regardless of the channel existence, as new channels with us will be added on handling of it, and channels we don't participate becomes new routes. All the other events **which have a `channel_identifier` argument** are the ones previously handled by the `payment_channel` filters, which registered a `[None, [channel_id]]` topic filter (which handled the events with `channel_identifier`/first argument as the `channel_identifier`s we care about aka we participate in. As all events are handled here, I kept a single, `[None]` event filter for the `TokenNetwork`, and filter out the channel specific events here.
